critic_raw:
The memory-guided anomaly detection paper makes several implicit claims that are not adequately tested.

1. The 'reduced representation capacity' mechanism is asserted but not isolated. The core claim is that the memory module forces the network to produce lower-quality reconstructions for anomalous inputs because it can only retrieve prototypical normal patterns. This mechanism is plausible but not verified directly: the paper does not show that reconstruction quality degrades specifically for anomaly inputs and not for hard-but-normal inputs (e.g., unusual normal frames). Without isolating this, it is possible that the memory module simply acts as a bottleneck that compresses all inputs, normal or not, producing a general degradation rather than a selective one. Evidence would be a controlled experiment comparing reconstruction error distributions for (a) anomalous frames, (b) visually complex normal frames, and (c) typical normal frames.

2. The hybrid PSNR + L2 abnormality score lacks principled justification. PSNR and L2 are not independent metrics — PSNR is a monotonic function of MSE in pixel space, so combining them yields redundant information unless applied to different signals (e.g., reconstruction vs. memory query residual). The paper does not report an ablation showing whether either component alone achieves the same or different performance. Without this, the weighting choice is arbitrary and the combined score may simply double-count the reconstruction error.

3. The Test-Time Updation strategy introduces a circularity risk. Updating memory items based on test-time observations assumes that test data is predominantly normal, which is precisely the condition the model is supposed to detect when violated. If anomaly rates in test streams are non-negligible, memory items will be contaminated by anomalous patterns, causing the threshold to drift. The paper does not report experiments with varying anomaly injection rates during test-time update, nor does it establish the sensitivity of classification accuracy to anomaly contamination in the update stream.

4. The Separateness and Compactness losses are evaluated only by their contribution to final AUC, not by whether the memory items they produce actually exhibit the claimed geometric properties. The paper claims these losses create diverse, well-separated memory representations, but does not report measures of inter-item distance or within-item compactness before and after training. Without measuring the representation geometry directly, the attribution of performance gains to these losses is confounded: the losses could be providing regularization benefits unrelated to memory diversity.

5. Evaluation on standard video anomaly benchmarks (e.g., UCSD Ped, CUHK Avenue, ShanghaiTech) does not disaggregate performance by anomaly type or by scene complexity. The reported AUC numbers aggregate over all anomaly types, some of which may be trivially detectable via any reconstruction model. This makes it impossible to assess whether the memory mechanism specifically enables detection of the hard cases (subtle, low-contrast, or semantically similar-to-normal anomalies) that motivate the design.
all_issues_raised: The selective reconstruction degradation mechanism is not isolated from general bottleneck compression effects
The hybrid PSNR + L2 abnormality score is redundant and lacks ablation justification
Test-time memory update assumes predominantly normal test streams, creating circularity when anomaly rates are non-negligible
Separateness and Compactness losses are not evaluated by direct measurement of memory geometry
AUC evaluation aggregates over anomaly types without disaggregating hard vs. easy cases
all_issues_adjudicated: The selective reconstruction degradation mechanism is not isolated from general bottleneck compression effects
The hybrid PSNR + L2 abnormality score is redundant and lacks ablation justification
Test-time memory update assumes predominantly normal test streams, creating circularity when anomaly rates are non-negligible
Separateness and Compactness losses are not evaluated by direct measurement of memory geometry
AUC evaluation aggregates over anomaly types without disaggregating hard vs. easy cases

critic_raw:
GAN2Shape's claim to recover 3D structure from a 2D GAN raises several methodological problems.

1. The method conflates GAN-internal consistency with geometric truth. GAN2Shape exploits the StyleGAN2 latent space to generate multiple viewpoints, but StyleGAN2 was trained for photorealism, not geometric consistency. The assumption that varying the latent vector w produces geometrically valid multi-view images of the same object is untested. StyleGAN2 can produce plausible-looking novel views while violating epipolar geometry, surface normal consistency, or depth ordering constraints. The paper does not verify that recovered 3D shapes are metrically consistent across views using any 3D reprojection error measure.

2. Evaluation on faces, cats, cars, and buildings conflates domain-specific results with general claims. Human faces have near-Lambertian surfaces, bilateral symmetry, and extensive training data priors — all properties that make reconstruction artificially easy. Results on faces do not predict performance on objects without these properties. The paper does not characterize how performance varies across domains or which domains drive the aggregate metrics.

3. Shape recovery is evaluated visually rather than against ground-truth 3D geometry. The paper reports no quantitative 3D shape accuracy metric (e.g., Chamfer distance, volumetric IoU, surface normal error against LiDAR or stereo ground truth). Visual inspection is insufficient because humans cannot reliably judge metric depth from rendered images, and the StyleGAN renderer can produce plausible-looking but metrically wrong shapes.

4. The disentanglement of shape from appearance in the StyleGAN2 latent space is assumed rather than demonstrated. The paper does not show that the w vector components it manipulates are causally responsible for viewpoint and lighting changes rather than correlated texture changes. If texture and geometry are entangled in the latent space (which is likely given that StyleGAN2 has no explicit 3D inductive bias), then the recovered shapes are partially texture artifacts projected into 3D, not genuine geometric reconstruction.

5. The replication study's 'novel shape priors' are introduced without a clean ablation against the original method. If the replicated model with novel priors achieves different quantitative results than the original, the delta could be due to implementation differences in the replication rather than the novel priors. Without an exact baseline replication step before adding priors, attribution is impossible.
all_issues_raised: StyleGAN2 latent variation does not guarantee geometrically valid multi-view consistency
Domain-specific results on faces and symmetric objects do not generalize to arbitrary object classes
No quantitative 3D ground-truth evaluation; shape accuracy assessed only visually
Shape-appearance disentanglement in StyleGAN2 latent space is assumed, not demonstrated
Novel shape priors in the replication are not ablated against an exact baseline replication
all_issues_adjudicated: StyleGAN2 latent variation does not guarantee geometrically valid multi-view consistency
Domain-specific results on faces and symmetric objects do not generalize to arbitrary object classes
No quantitative 3D ground-truth evaluation; shape accuracy assessed only visually
Shape-appearance disentanglement in StyleGAN2 latent space is assumed, not demonstrated
Novel shape priors in the replication are not ablated against an exact baseline replication

critic_raw:
The privacy-preserving transformation search paper has several methodological gaps.

1. The privacy score S_pri is defined against specific reconstruction attack instances used during search, making the metric circular with the defense. The transformation policy is optimized to defeat the attacks that S_pri measures — this is not a demonstration of general privacy preservation, it is a demonstration of goodness-of-fit to the attack set used in the search. The paper does not evaluate whether discovered policies generalize to held-out reconstruction attacks not seen during policy search. This is the central validity threat: a policy can score perfectly on S_pri while remaining vulnerable to any attack not in the search set.

2. The claim that policies have 'negligible impact on training efficiency' requires a precise definition of efficiency. If efficiency means wall-clock training time, the data augmentation pipeline adds latency. If it means final model accuracy, the comparison baseline must be a model trained on unaugmented data with identical hyperparameters. The paper does not report confidence intervals on S_acc across multiple training runs, making it impossible to distinguish 'negligible' degradation from within-run variance.

3. Transferability of policies across datasets is tested empirically but not explained mechanistically. If a policy found on dataset A transfers to dataset B, this could be because both datasets share the same low-level statistical structure (e.g., similar pixel value distributions), not because the policy provides semantic-content-preserving obfuscation as claimed. The paper does not control for dataset similarity when reporting cross-dataset transfer results.

4. The augmentation search space is finite and discrete, but real reconstruction attacks can adapt to known augmentation strategies. The paper treats reconstruction attacks as fixed adversaries during evaluation, but a motivated attacker with knowledge of the deployed augmentation policy can train an attack-specific deaugmentation network. The paper does not test adaptive attacks that model the augmentation distribution.

5. The federated learning threat model is underspecified. The paper assumes gradients are the attack surface but does not specify whether the adversary is honest-but-curious (observes gradients) or active (can modify model updates). The strength of the privacy guarantee changes fundamentally depending on the threat model, and the S_pri metric is calibrated to only one of these settings.
all_issues_raised: S_pri is circular — evaluated only against attacks used during policy search, not held-out attacks
Negligible accuracy impact claim lacks confidence intervals and precise efficiency definition
Cross-dataset transferability attributed to semantic generalization without controlling for dataset similarity
Attack model is non-adaptive; policies may be broken by attackers with knowledge of the augmentation strategy
Federated learning threat model is underspecified (honest-but-curious vs. active adversary)
all_issues_adjudicated: S_pri is circular — evaluated only against attacks used during policy search, not held-out attacks
Negligible accuracy impact claim lacks confidence intervals and precise efficiency definition
Cross-dataset transferability attributed to semantic generalization without controlling for dataset similarity
Attack model is non-adaptive; policies may be broken by attackers with knowledge of the augmentation strategy
Federated learning threat model is underspecified (honest-but-curious vs. active adversary)

critic_raw:
The learned-reference phase retrieval paper has several validity concerns.

1. The generalization claim — that the learned reference generalizes to unseen data distributions — is evaluated only on data drawn from the same source as training (open-access datasets of the same modality). True distribution shift would require testing on a different measurement modality (e.g., training on natural images, testing on optical microscopy data). Without this, 'generalizes to unseen data distributions' means 'generalizes to held-out samples from the same distribution,' which is a much weaker claim than stated.

2. Robustness to spatial augmentations (shifting and rotation) is claimed but the mechanism is not explained. If the reference is learned jointly with the reconstruction network, it is possible that the network learns to be invariant to the augmentations applied during training, which is standard data augmentation, not evidence of genuine robustness. The claim would require testing on augmentations not included in the training distribution.

3. The unrolled network architecture has a fixed number of iterations (unrolling depth), which acts as an implicit regularizer. The paper does not report how performance varies with unrolling depth or whether the learned reference is stable across different depths. If performance peaks at a specific depth and degrades otherwise, the 'learned reference' may be tuned to a specific algorithmic trajectory rather than representing a generally useful prior.

4. The comparison baselines in phase retrieval are classical algorithms (e.g., HIO, error reduction) rather than recent deep learning approaches. This understates the competitive landscape and makes it impossible to assess whether the learned reference provides benefit over a standard learned initialization strategy that does not use the reference construction.

5. The measurement model assumes noiseless Fourier magnitude measurements. Real optical systems have photon shot noise, detector read noise, and quantization. The paper does not test robustness to measurement noise, which is the primary challenge in physical phase retrieval applications. A method that works on clean simulated measurements may fail at moderate SNR levels encountered in practice.
all_issues_raised: Generalization to unseen distributions is tested on held-out same-distribution data, not genuine distribution shift
Robustness to spatial augmentations may reflect training-time augmentation invariance rather than genuine robustness
Unrolling depth is not ablated; learned reference may be tuned to a specific algorithmic trajectory
Baselines are classical algorithms only; no comparison to recent deep learning phase retrieval methods
Noiseless measurement assumption not tested under realistic photon/detector noise conditions
all_issues_adjudicated: Generalization to unseen distributions is tested on held-out same-distribution data, not genuine distribution shift
Robustness to spatial augmentations may reflect training-time augmentation invariance rather than genuine robustness
Unrolling depth is not ablated; learned reference may be tuned to a specific algorithmic trajectory
Baselines are classical algorithms only; no comparison to recent deep learning phase retrieval methods
Noiseless measurement assumption not tested under realistic photon/detector noise conditions

critic_raw:
Hamiltonian Generative Networks makes strong physics claims that are not adequately tested.

1. The model is validated only on systems with very low-dimensional Hamiltonians (pendulum, mass-spring, two-body). These systems have well-known analytical solutions and relatively simple image dynamics. The claim that the model 'learns Hamiltonian dynamics without restrictive domain assumptions' is not supported by any experiment on systems where the true Hamiltonian is non-trivial or multi-modal. The omission of harder physical systems (e.g., coupled oscillators, chaotic systems) makes the evaluation domain much narrower than the claim.

2. The variational objective encourages reconstruction but does not enforce Hamiltonian structure directly. The model learns to reconstruct image sequences, and the Hamiltonian is a latent variable. There is no guarantee that the learned latent dynamics actually respect Hamilton's equations (energy conservation, symplecticity) unless these are explicitly enforced as constraints or regularizers. The paper should report whether the learned trajectories conserve a learned energy function over long rollouts.

3. Forward-and-backward rollout capability is presented as a feature (time reversibility), but time reversibility is a property of Hamiltonian mechanics specifically because of symplectic integration. The paper does not verify that the learned rollout operator is symplectic or that energy is conserved over long rollouts. Without this, 'backward rollout works' is evidence of smooth latent dynamics, not specifically Hamiltonian dynamics.

4. The image-to-trajectory encoder introduces an information bottleneck between pixel space and the latent Hamiltonian space. The paper does not report how much of the observed reconstruction quality depends on the encoder's ability to extract low-level motion features (e.g., background subtraction, optical flow) versus the HGN's actual learning of physics. A strong encoder with a trivial dynamics model might achieve similar reconstruction metrics.

5. Evaluation uses reconstruction error (pixel-level MSE or SSIM) on the training system types. This metric is dominated by low-frequency image content and background pixels, not by the correctness of trajectory dynamics. A model that correctly reconstructs the background but gets the trajectory wrong can achieve good reconstruction metrics. The paper does not report trajectory-level accuracy (e.g., position/velocity MSE in the latent or pixel space over time).
all_issues_raised: Evaluation only on simple low-dimensional systems; no experiments on non-trivial or chaotic systems
Variational objective does not enforce Hamiltonian structure (energy conservation, symplecticity) explicitly
Time reversibility is presented as evidence of Hamiltonian learning but is not verified by symplecticity tests
Reconstruction quality may be dominated by encoder quality rather than learned physics
Pixel-level reconstruction metrics are insensitive to trajectory correctness
all_issues_adjudicated: Evaluation only on simple low-dimensional systems; no experiments on non-trivial or chaotic systems
Variational objective does not enforce Hamiltonian structure (energy conservation, symplecticity) explicitly
Time reversibility is presented as evidence of Hamiltonian learning but is not verified by symplecticity tests
Reconstruction quality may be dominated by encoder quality rather than learned physics
Pixel-level reconstruction metrics are insensitive to trajectory correctness

critic_raw:
Graph Edit Networks (GEN) has a cluster of evaluation and generalization issues.

1. All experiments use synthetic datasets generated specifically for the GEN evaluation. This is a significant validity threat: the synthetic data is designed to match the GEN's inductive biases (e.g., graph edits of specific types and sizes), and there is no guarantee that real-world graph transformation tasks have the same structural properties. The paper does not test on a single real-world graph transformation benchmark (e.g., molecular reaction prediction, program synthesis, knowledge graph editing).

2. The scalability claim — that GEN handles larger graphs within reasonable computational limits — is supported by runtime experiments on synthetic graphs of increasing size, but the synthetic graphs likely have uniform degree distributions. Real-world graphs (e.g., social networks, biological networks) have power-law degree distributions, which create highly variable local neighborhoods. The paper does not test scalability on graphs with heterogeneous topology.

3. GEN is an output layer, so its performance depends critically on the quality of the upstream node and edge representations produced by the GNN backbone. The paper does not ablate GEN across different GNN backbones (e.g., GCN, GAT, MPNN). If GEN only works well with one specific backbone, the architectural claim is narrower than presented.

4. The 'learned operations' for graph editing (node insertion, deletion, edge modification) require predicting a structured output that is exponentially large in the number of graph nodes. The paper does not discuss how GEN handles the combinatorial explosion of possible edit sequences for graphs beyond small toy sizes, and whether the greedy decoding strategy produces globally optimal edits or locally optimal ones that diverge from ground truth.

5. Evaluation metrics for graph transformation tasks are not standardized. The paper appears to use task-specific accuracy on synthetic data without reporting graph edit distance between predicted and target graphs. Graph edit distance would provide a continuous measure of how close the output is to the correct transformation, revealing whether GEN makes many small errors or few large ones.
all_issues_raised: Evaluation is exclusively on synthetic data designed to match GEN's inductive biases; no real-world benchmark testing
Scalability tested on uniform-degree synthetic graphs, not power-law topologies characteristic of real networks
GEN performance is not ablated across different GNN backbones
Combinatorial explosion of edit sequences not addressed; greedy decoding may diverge from global optimum
No graph edit distance metric reported; task-specific accuracy on synthetic data is insufficient
all_issues_adjudicated: Evaluation is exclusively on synthetic data designed to match GEN's inductive biases; no real-world benchmark testing
Scalability tested on uniform-degree synthetic graphs, not power-law topologies characteristic of real networks
GEN performance is not ablated across different GNN backbones
Combinatorial explosion of edit sequences not addressed; greedy decoding may diverge from global optimum
No graph edit distance metric reported; task-specific accuracy on synthetic data is insufficient

critic_raw:
Generative Causal Explanations (GCE) for black-box classifiers has several methodological weaknesses.

1. The causal/non-causal factor distinction is grounded in the GCE model's own latent space structure, not in a ground-truth causal graph. The paper cannot validate that the factors it labels 'causal' are causally responsible for classifier decisions in the interventional sense without access to ground-truth labels of causal factors (which are not available for arbitrary classifiers). On MNIST and fMNIST, a proxy for causal factors can be constructed (e.g., digit class vs. background), but this proxy is not used to validate the GCE's factorization.

2. The experiments are confined to MNIST and fMNIST, which are low-dimensional, near-binary images with limited background variation. The claim that the framework generalizes to 'black-box classifiers' is not supported — it is supported for classifiers operating on low-dimensional datasets with highly structured visual content. Whether GCE's latent factor structure is identifiable for natural images or tabular data is not tested.

3. The dependency on a specific generative model architecture (VAE or GAN) means that the quality of causal explanations is entangled with the quality of the generative model. If the generative model fails to represent a factor faithfully (e.g., a face attribute that requires high spatial frequency), the GCE will report that factor as non-causal even if it is causally relevant. The paper does not measure generative model quality independently from explanation quality.

4. The replication study's account of computational hardware (GTX 1080 GPUs) is stated as if it establishes reproducibility, but hardware specification does not ensure numerical determinism. Floating-point non-determinism in GPU operations and non-deterministic CUDNN kernels can produce divergent results across runs. The replication does not report variance across seeds or document measures taken to ensure reproducibility beyond hardware matching.

5. The evaluation of 'causal explanations' uses no user study or downstream utility measure. Explanation quality is assessed by the investigators' visual inspection of generated counterfactual images, which is susceptible to confirmation bias. A proper evaluation would measure whether the explanations improve user understanding of the classifier's decision boundary on held-out examples, or whether they predict classifier behavior on novel inputs.
all_issues_raised: Causal/non-causal factor distinction relies on latent space structure, not ground-truth causal graphs
Evaluation limited to MNIST/fMNIST; generalization to arbitrary black-box classifiers is not tested
Explanation quality is entangled with generative model quality; independent assessment is missing
Hardware specification does not ensure reproducibility; variance across seeds is not reported
No user study or downstream utility measure for explanation quality; visual inspection is susceptible to confirmation bias
all_issues_adjudicated: Causal/non-causal factor distinction relies on latent space structure, not ground-truth causal graphs
Evaluation limited to MNIST/fMNIST; generalization to arbitrary black-box classifiers is not tested
Explanation quality is entangled with generative model quality; independent assessment is missing
Hardware specification does not ensure reproducibility; variance across seeds is not reported
No user study or downstream utility measure for explanation quality; visual inspection is susceptible to confirmation bias

critic_raw:
YNet's trajectory forecasting methodology has several important gaps.

1. The two-stage goal-then-path prediction design introduces a compounding error problem: if the goal prediction in stage one is wrong, the path prediction in stage two is conditioned on a wrong goal. The paper reports aggregate ADE/FDE metrics but does not decompose performance into goal prediction error versus conditional path prediction error given a correct goal. This decomposition is essential for understanding whether the model's strength is in goal prediction or in path interpolation.

2. The use of segmentation maps as an input implies ground-truth scene understanding at inference time. Real deployment scenarios may require the segmentation maps themselves to be predicted, introducing segmentation errors that the paper does not account for. The paper does not test YNet with imperfect or predicted segmentation maps, making the evaluation overoptimistic relative to deployment conditions.

3. The datasets (SDD and ETH/UCY) have known distribution characteristics that may favor waypoint-based methods. SDD contains many trajectories with clear goal structure (e.g., entering buildings, reaching specific spots), and ETH/UCY pedestrian trajectories are relatively short-horizon. The claim that waypoint conditioning helps in general does not follow from results on these specific datasets without a dataset where long-range goal structure is absent or misleading.

4. The multi-modal prediction is evaluated using the best-of-K (oracle) ADE/FDE metric, which selects the closest prediction to ground truth from K samples. This metric rewards sample diversity but not calibration — a model that produces K widely spread trajectories will score well even if no individual trajectory is a good prediction of the actual future. The paper does not report calibration metrics (e.g., NLL, or what fraction of ground-truth trajectories fall within a predicted distribution contour).

5. The model claims to address both epistemic factors (intent) and aleatoric factors (environmental unpredictability), but these are both handled by the same stochastic sampling mechanism from the probability maps. There is no separate modeling or quantification of each source of uncertainty. Without disentangling these, it is impossible to assess whether the model has learned genuine intent representations or is simply memorizing trajectory patterns from the training set.
all_issues_raised: Compounding goal-then-path error not decomposed; aggregate ADE/FDE obscures which stage fails
Ground-truth segmentation maps used at inference; no testing with predicted or imperfect segmentations
SDD and ETH/UCY have goal-structured trajectories that may bias results in favor of waypoint methods
Best-of-K oracle metric rewards diversity not calibration; calibration metrics absent
Epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty are not disentangled; both handled by same stochastic mechanism
all_issues_adjudicated: Compounding goal-then-path error not decomposed; aggregate ADE/FDE obscures which stage fails
Ground-truth segmentation maps used at inference; no testing with predicted or imperfect segmentations
SDD and ETH/UCY have goal-structured trajectories that may bias results in favor of waypoint methods
Best-of-K oracle metric rewards diversity not calibration; calibration metrics absent
Epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty are not disentangled; both handled by same stochastic mechanism

critic_raw:
Strategic Classification Made Practical makes several implicit claims that are not adequately tested.

1. The framework assumes that the strategic response model (how users game the classifier) is known or can be estimated during training. In practice, the true strategic response function is unknown and must be approximated from observed behavior. The paper does not report experiments where the assumed response model is misspecified relative to the actual user behavior, which is the realistic deployment setting. Robustness to response model misspecification is a core practical requirement.

2. The regularization strategy for 'favorable social outcomes' is evaluated on specific benchmark scenarios, but social outcomes are defined by the choice of regularizer. Different regularizers produce different notions of 'favorable.' The paper does not report sensitivity analysis showing how different regularization choices affect outcome fairness versus classifier accuracy. Without this, the regularization recommendation is not actionable — practitioners would need to know which regularizer to use in their specific context.

3. The end-to-end trainable framework requires differentiating through the strategic user's best response, which is typically non-differentiable. The paper presumably uses some relaxation or approximation for this differentiation. The quality of this approximation — i.e., how well the gradient surrogate tracks the true gradient — is not reported. A poor surrogate could cause the training to converge to a local minimum that is not strategically robust.

4. The comparison to 'non-strategic approaches' does not account for the fact that non-strategic classifiers trained on historically strategic data implicitly model some strategic behavior. If the training data was collected under strategic incentives, then even a standard ERM classifier has absorbed some strategic response signal. The paper should compare against ERM trained on non-strategic data as a cleaner baseline.

5. The flexibility claim — that the framework works across 'a variety of real-life scenarios' — is supported by experiments on a small number of datasets (likely standard tabular benchmarks). Whether the strategic response assumptions hold in these datasets (i.e., whether users in these domains actually behave strategically in the modeled way) is not validated through behavioral data or economic theory.
all_issues_raised: Strategic response model assumed known at training time; misspecification robustness not tested
Regularization choice for social outcomes not subjected to sensitivity analysis
Differentiating through best-response requires non-differentiable relaxation; gradient surrogate quality not reported
Non-strategic baseline trained on historically strategic data conflates comparison
Strategic behavior assumption for benchmark datasets not validated against actual user behavior
all_issues_adjudicated: Strategic response model assumed known at training time; misspecification robustness not tested
Regularization choice for social outcomes not subjected to sensitivity analysis
Differentiating through best-response requires non-differentiable relaxation; gradient surrogate quality not reported
Non-strategic baseline trained on historically strategic data conflates comparison
Strategic behavior assumption for benchmark datasets not validated against actual user behavior

critic_raw:
The ATSPrivacy framework for privacy-preserving collaborative learning has similar issues to the Drabent replication (rc_rescience_2021_drabent2022replication_journal) but with distinct additional concerns.

1. The privacy score metric is defined against specific reconstruction attacks that are used during the augmentation policy search. This creates a closed loop: the policy is optimized against the attacks that score it, and there is no guarantee of privacy against novel reconstruction methods. The evaluation should include at minimum one held-out attack not used during search to test generalization of the privacy guarantee.

2. The policy search treats privacy and accuracy as two objectives to be balanced via score thresholds. However, the Pareto frontier of privacy-accuracy tradeoffs is not characterized. The paper reports a single policy per experiment, but there may be policies that achieve substantially better privacy at a small accuracy cost, or vice versa. Without the full Pareto frontier, it is impossible to know whether the selected policy represents a good operating point.

3. The augmentation operations are applied locally before gradient computation. This means the gradients themselves carry information about the augmented (not original) image. A sophisticated reconstruction attack that knows the augmentation policy can apply the inverse transformation to approximately reconstruct the original image. The paper does not test this 'white-box augmentation' attack scenario.

4. The framework's effectiveness depends on the assumption that gradient-based reconstruction attacks cannot invert the augmentation pipeline. For simple augmentations like translations and contrast adjustments, analytical inversion may be possible. For more complex augmentations, learned inversion may be feasible. The paper treats the augmentation as a one-way function without establishing that it has one-way properties under any formal threat model.

5. The accuracy score S_acc is measured on a specific model and dataset pair. It is possible that augmentations that have negligible impact on one architecture (e.g., ResNet) have significant impact on another (e.g., attention-based models that are more sensitive to local spatial transformations). Cross-architecture generalization of the accuracy score is not reported.
all_issues_raised: Privacy score evaluated only against attacks used in policy search; no held-out attack evaluation
Pareto frontier of privacy-accuracy tradeoffs not characterized; single operating point reported
White-box augmentation attack (attacker knows policy, applies inverse transform) not tested
Augmentation is not established as a one-way function under any formal threat model
Accuracy score generalization across architectures not tested
all_issues_adjudicated: Privacy score evaluated only against attacks used in policy search; no held-out attack evaluation
Pareto frontier of privacy-accuracy tradeoffs not characterized; single operating point reported
White-box augmentation attack (attacker knows policy, applies inverse transform) not tested
Augmentation is not established as a one-way function under any formal threat model
Accuracy score generalization across architectures not tested

critic_raw:
The truncated SVD update algorithm has several validity concerns.

1. The computational efficiency claims depend critically on the ratio of update size to matrix size. For small updates to large matrices, an incremental method avoids full recomputation. But for large updates or for matrices that change structurally (e.g., rank changes, large entry perturbations), the projection-based approach may be no better than full recomputation. The paper should report how performance degrades as a function of update fraction, not just on small-update scenarios where the method is designed to excel.

2. The accuracy of the approximation is measured against the exact truncated SVD, but the truncated SVD is itself an approximation of the full SVD. The relevant question is whether the approximation error introduced by the incremental update is smaller than the inherent truncation error. The paper does not report the combined approximation error (truncation + update approximation) relative to ground truth, which would reveal the practical impact of the incremental approach.

3. The theoretical analysis likely assumes specific conditions on the perturbation structure (e.g., rank-1 or low-rank updates). Real evolving matrices in applications such as recommendation systems or streaming PCA have update structures that may violate these assumptions. The paper does not report empirical results on realistic update distributions from production systems.

4. The baseline comparison is against naive full recomputation, which is the weakest possible baseline. There are multiple established incremental SVD methods (e.g., Brand 2006, Levy & Goldberg 2014) with known complexity bounds. Comparing only against full recomputation does not establish that the proposed method is the best incremental approach.

5. The replication study does not appear to test the algorithm on dynamic systems where the matrix changes continuously and errors accumulate over time. A single-step update experiment does not reveal whether approximation errors compound over many sequential updates, which is the relevant scenario for the 'evolving matrices' framing.
all_issues_raised: Efficiency claims not tested as a function of update fraction; method may degrade for large updates
Combined truncation and update approximation error not reported against ground truth
Theoretical analysis likely assumes low-rank update structure not representative of real evolving matrices
Baseline is naive full recomputation; established incremental SVD methods not compared
No long-horizon sequential update experiment to test error accumulation over time
all_issues_adjudicated: Efficiency claims not tested as a function of update fraction; method may degrade for large updates
Combined truncation and update approximation error not reported against ground truth
Theoretical analysis likely assumes low-rank update structure not representative of real evolving matrices
Baseline is naive full recomputation; established incremental SVD methods not compared
No long-horizon sequential update experiment to test error accumulation over time

critic_raw:
Explaining in Style (StyleSpace for GAN-based classifier explanation) has several methodological issues.

1. The StyleSpace of StyleGAN2 is high-dimensional and the method must identify which style channels are causally relevant to the classifier. However, correlation between style channels and classifier outputs does not establish causality. The method likely identifies style directions that correlate with classifier decisions, but these correlations may reflect dataset biases (e.g., the classifier uses background texture to predict age) rather than semantically meaningful attributes. The paper does not test whether the identified style channels causally affect classifier outputs via intervention (i.e., do-calculus or randomized style manipulation).

2. The method is evaluated on a GAN trained on specific face datasets, where StyleGAN2's disentanglement is known to be high-quality. Whether the explanation method extends to GANs trained on less structured data (e.g., natural scenes, medical images) where StyleSpace may be less disentangled is not tested.

3. The evaluation of explanation quality is typically done through visual inspection of generated counterfactuals. This is not a controlled evaluation: different raters may perceive different attributes as causally relevant. A rigorous evaluation would measure whether the identified causal attributes predict classifier decisions on independently generated images not used in the explanation procedure.

4. The disentanglement in StyleSpace may be partially an artifact of the training process: StyleGAN2's style mixing regularization encourages style channels to be independent, but this independence is in the generative sense, not in the semantic sense relevant to the classifier. A channel that is generatively independent of others may still be semantically entangled (e.g., a channel that controls both skin color and lighting simultaneously).

5. The replication study should verify that the identified explanatory style channels are stable across multiple random seeds for GAN inversion (projecting real images into StyleSpace). If the style code depends sensitively on initialization, the explanation is not reproducible for the same input image, which would undermine the method's practical utility.
all_issues_raised: Correlation between style channels and classifier outputs does not establish causal relevance; intervention not tested
Method only evaluated on face GANs with high StyleSpace disentanglement; generalization to other domains not tested
Explanation quality evaluated by visual inspection without controlled rater study or independent prediction test
StyleGAN2 generative independence of style channels does not imply semantic independence relevant to the classifier
Stability of identified style channels across GAN inversion seeds not verified
all_issues_adjudicated: Correlation between style channels and classifier outputs does not establish causal relevance; intervention not tested
Method only evaluated on face GANs with high StyleSpace disentanglement; generalization to other domains not tested
Explanation quality evaluated by visual inspection without controlled rater study or independent prediction test
StyleGAN2 generative independence of style channels does not imply semantic independence relevant to the classifier
Stability of identified style channels across GAN inversion seeds not verified

critic_raw:
DECAF's causally-aware debiasing GAN has several fundamental validity concerns.

1. The causal DAG used to guide DECAF's generation must be specified by the practitioner. This DAG encodes assumptions about which variables are causes and which are effects of the sensitive attribute. If the DAG is misspecified — which is likely in practice because true causal structures are rarely known — DECAF will systematically generate biased synthetic data while appearing to apply causal debiasing. The paper does not report sensitivity analysis to DAG misspecification.

2. The 'bias removal' is defined relative to the specified DAG, but fairness and bias have multiple definitions (demographic parity, equalized odds, individual fairness). DECAF's causal intervention removes one type of bias but may introduce or worsen other types. The paper evaluates fairness using specific metrics without showing that improving those metrics does not degrade other fairness criteria.

3. The evaluation measures fairness of downstream models trained on DECAF-generated synthetic data, but downstream model choice is not ablated. If fairness metrics are measured only on one specific downstream classifier architecture and training procedure, the results may not generalize to other classifiers. The selection of downstream model is an undisclosed degree of freedom.

4. The synthetic data is compared to real data using distributional metrics (e.g., statistical similarity), but distributional similarity does not imply that the synthetic data preserves the joint structure needed for valid fairness evaluation. Specifically, if DECAF breaks the correlational structure between non-sensitive features while removing the sensitive attribute's causal effect, downstream models trained on synthetic data may learn spurious patterns not present in the real distribution.

5. The GAN training instability problem is generic to GAN-based approaches. DECAF's causal conditioning adds an additional complexity layer where the GAN must simultaneously learn to match the causal structure and produce realistic outputs. The paper does not report how often training fails to converge, or what the variance in fairness metrics is across multiple GAN training runs.
all_issues_raised: Causal DAG must be manually specified; sensitivity to DAG misspecification not tested
Bias removal optimized for one fairness definition may worsen other fairness criteria
Downstream classifier choice is an undisclosed degree of freedom in fairness evaluation
Distributional similarity of synthetic data does not guarantee preservation of joint structure for fairness evaluation
GAN training instability and variance in fairness metrics across runs not reported
all_issues_adjudicated: Causal DAG must be manually specified; sensitivity to DAG misspecification not tested
Bias removal optimized for one fairness definition may worsen other fairness criteria
Downstream classifier choice is an undisclosed degree of freedom in fairness evaluation
Distributional similarity of synthetic data does not guarantee preservation of joint structure for fairness evaluation
GAN training instability and variance in fairness metrics across runs not reported

critic_raw:
The 'Rigging the Lottery' (SNIP/GraSP/RigL-style sparse training) reproducibility study has several important issues.

1. The 'universal applicability' claim requires that the method works across all architectures tested, but sparse training methods are known to be highly sensitive to architecture choice — specifically to the ratio of parameter budget allocated to different layer types. If some architectures in the study are more amenable to sparsity (e.g., overparameterized ResNets) and others are not (e.g., compact MobileNets), averaging results across architectures obscures architecture-specific failures. The paper should report per-architecture results and variance.

2. The reproduced results may differ from the original due to hardware differences (GPU generation, memory bandwidth) that affect floating-point accumulation order. The replication study should report whether numerical differences in reproduced metrics fall within the original paper's stated confidence intervals, or whether the deviations are statistically significant. Without this comparison, 'replication' is ambiguous — it could mean obtaining qualitatively similar results but not quantitatively matching ones.

3. Sparse training methods in this family typically use a dynamic mask update schedule (e.g., cosine annealing for the update rate). The replication's fidelity to this schedule is critical: a slightly different annealing schedule can produce substantially different final accuracy. The paper should document exactly which hyperparameters were re-used verbatim and which were independently tuned.

4. The 'statistical analyses to ascertain improvements over standard initialization' require that the comparison baseline (standard initialization + dense training) is trained to convergence with the same computational budget as the sparse method. Sparse training methods often use the same number of training steps as dense training but with different per-step cost. If the comparison is steps-equal rather than FLOP-equal, the sparse method may have an implicit advantage or disadvantage depending on the architecture.

5. The reproducibility study presumably operates on the original authors' code. Code-based reproduction conflates the algorithm with its implementation — subtle implementation choices (weight initialization scheme, gradient clipping thresholds, learning rate warmup) may account for performance differences that the paper attributes to the algorithm. These implementation details should be catalogued and their impact isolated.
all_issues_raised: Universal applicability claim obscures architecture-specific performance variation
Numerical deviations from original results not compared against original confidence intervals
Fidelity of sparse mask update schedule in replication not documented
Steps-equal vs. FLOP-equal comparison baseline conflates computational budget
Code-based reproduction conflates algorithmic with implementation-level contributions
all_issues_adjudicated: Universal applicability claim obscures architecture-specific performance variation
Numerical deviations from original results not compared against original confidence intervals
Fidelity of sparse mask update schedule in replication not documented
Steps-equal vs. FLOP-equal comparison baseline conflates computational budget
Code-based reproduction conflates algorithmic with implementation-level contributions

critic_raw:
Counterfactual Generative Networks (CGN) has several evaluation and conceptual issues.

1. The counterfactual conditioning mechanism relies on 'rich prior knowledge sources' for sampling counterfactual contexts. The quality and coverage of these priors directly determine whether the generated samples are meaningful counterfactuals or merely out-of-distribution noise. The paper does not report how sensitive generation quality is to the prior specification, or what happens when the prior is uninformative or adversarially chosen.

2. FID and IS are the primary evaluation metrics, but both measure distributional properties of generated images, not counterfactual validity. A counterfactual is valid if it represents the minimal change to the input that would have changed the conditioning label. FID measures closeness of marginal distributions, not conditional coherence. The paper should evaluate whether generated samples are plausible counterfactuals by measuring classifier confidence or semantic distance from the original input under the new condition.

3. The conditional GAN is conditioned on both real inputs and counterfactual contexts. If the counterfactual context is sampled from a prior that is correlated with the training data distribution, the model may learn to ignore the counterfactual conditioning and simply generate from the marginal distribution. The paper should report a conditioning ablation showing that removing the counterfactual context degrades output quality or diversity.

4. The claim that CGN improves diversity of generated samples is measured relative to a baseline GAN. However, diversity is only meaningful if the diverse samples are valid — i.e., counterfactually plausible. A model that generates high-diversity but implausible counterfactuals would score well on diversity metrics while failing at the application. The paper does not measure plausibility of individual generated samples.

5. The reproducibility study must verify that the counterfactual conditioning mechanism is actually active during inference, not just during training. In practice, GAN generators can collapse to ignoring conditional inputs (mode collapse on the conditioning variable). The replication should include a diagnostic showing that the generator's output distribution varies measurably with the counterfactual context input.
all_issues_raised: Counterfactual prior quality and coverage not characterized; sensitivity to prior specification not reported
FID and IS measure distributional properties, not counterfactual validity
Conditioning ablation missing; model may learn to ignore counterfactual context if correlated with training distribution
Sample diversity metric does not account for counterfactual plausibility
Conditional mode collapse (generator ignoring counterfactual input) not diagnosed in replication
all_issues_adjudicated: Counterfactual prior quality and coverage not characterized; sensitivity to prior specification not reported
FID and IS measure distributional properties, not counterfactual validity
Conditioning ablation missing; model may learn to ignore counterfactual context if correlated with training distribution
Sample diversity metric does not account for counterfactual plausibility
Conditional mode collapse (generator ignoring counterfactual input) not diagnosed in replication

critic_raw:
Training Binary Neural Networks with the Bayesian Learning Rule has several fundamental concerns.

1. The Bayesian Learning Rule applied to binary weights requires a tractable posterior approximation, since the true posterior over {-1, 1} weights is intractable for deep networks. The approximation quality — and specifically, whether the approximate posterior captures multi-modality in the weight space — is not directly evaluated. A unimodal approximation over a fundamentally discrete, multi-modal space would provide poor uncertainty quantification despite formally using a Bayesian framework.

2. The 'improved uncertainty quantification' claim requires a calibration evaluation (e.g., reliability diagrams, ECE) on held-out data. Bayesian methods can be overconfident when the approximation is poor or when the prior is misspecified. The paper presumably reports predictive accuracy improvements but it is not established that the uncertainty estimates are calibrated in the sense that 70% confidence predictions are correct 70% of the time.

3. Computational efficiency for resource-constrained environments requires a comparison on actual hardware (e.g., ARM processors, FPGAs) where binary operations provide measurable speedup over floating-point operations. Reporting training efficiency on GPU does not establish inference efficiency on deployment hardware. Binary operations on CUDA GPUs may not be faster than float16 due to GPU memory bandwidth characteristics.

4. The Bayesian Learning Rule is derived under specific assumptions about the loss landscape curvature (e.g., via a natural gradient or Fisher information approximation). For binary networks, the gradient of the loss with respect to binary weights is undefined, requiring a straight-through estimator or similar surrogate. The paper should report whether the Bayesian update rule remains principled under the surrogate gradient, or whether the Bayesian interpretation breaks down at this point.

5. The comparison to non-Bayesian BNN training methods (e.g., XNOR-Net, BinaryConnect) must control for the number of weight samples used at inference. Bayesian methods often average over multiple weight samples to improve predictions. If the non-Bayesian baseline uses a single weight set and the Bayesian method uses multiple samples, the comparison is not fair in terms of inference cost.
all_issues_raised: Posterior approximation quality over discrete binary weights not evaluated; multi-modality may be lost
Uncertainty calibration not evaluated; predictive accuracy improvement does not imply well-calibrated posteriors
Computational efficiency claim based on GPU training, not inference on binary-hardware targets
Bayesian Learning Rule derivation may break down under surrogate gradients required for binary weights
Inference cost not controlled when comparing Bayesian (multi-sample) vs. non-Bayesian (single-sample) methods
all_issues_adjudicated: Posterior approximation quality over discrete binary weights not evaluated; multi-modality may be lost
Uncertainty calibration not evaluated; predictive accuracy improvement does not imply well-calibrated posteriors
Computational efficiency claim based on GPU training, not inference on binary-hardware targets
Bayesian Learning Rule derivation may break down under surrogate gradients required for binary weights
Inference cost not controlled when comparing Bayesian (multi-sample) vs. non-Bayesian (single-sample) methods

critic_raw:
SCOUTER's slot-attention-based explainability method has several methodological weaknesses.

1. The claim that SCOUTER's explanations 'outperform those by other methods on several explanation evaluation metrics' requires that the metrics themselves are valid measures of explanation quality. Standard explanation metrics (e.g., insertion/deletion, pointing game accuracy) have known failure modes: a model can score well by highlighting discriminative pixels that are not causally relevant to the classification. The paper does not validate that the explanation metrics it uses are aligned with human-interpretable causal relevance.

2. SCOUTER's slot attention module generates spatial attention maps that are used as explanations. But the attention map and the classification decision are produced by the same forward pass through the network. There is no guarantee that the attention weights reflect the features the classifier uses — slot attention is designed for object segmentation, and the attention-to-decision pathway may not be faithfully captured by the attention weights when the network is fine-tuned for classification.

3. The 'positive and negative visual explanations' generated by SCOUTER are evaluated on specific datasets. If the datasets have class-discriminative features that happen to be spatially localized (e.g., bird species with distinctive head markings), SCOUTER's slot-based spatial explanations will appear more accurate than methods without slot structure. The evaluation should include datasets where discriminative features are globally distributed to test whether slot attention provides genuine improvement.

4. The comparison with a 'fully connected model' for classification accuracy uses confusion matrix metrics for binary classification. Confusion matrix metrics (precision, recall, F1) are threshold-dependent, and the threshold choice for each model can significantly affect the comparison. The paper should report performance across the full ROC curve, not at a single operating threshold.

5. The replication uses 'consistent hyperparameters where specified.' This qualifier indicates that some hyperparameters were not specified in the original paper and had to be independently chosen by the replicators. These independently chosen hyperparameters are undisclosed degrees of freedom that could inflate or deflate replication fidelity. The replication should clearly document every hyperparameter that was not specified and the replicators' choices.
all_issues_raised: Explanation evaluation metrics may not measure causal relevance; metric validity not established
Attention weights and classification decision share same forward pass; attention may not faithfully reflect classifier reasoning
Evaluation datasets may have spatially localized discriminative features that favor slot-based methods
Confusion matrix metrics are threshold-dependent; full ROC curve not reported
Unspecified hyperparameters in original paper are undisclosed degrees of freedom in replication
all_issues_adjudicated: Explanation evaluation metrics may not measure causal relevance; metric validity not established
Attention weights and classification decision share same forward pass; attention may not faithfully reflect classifier reasoning
Evaluation datasets may have spatially localized discriminative features that favor slot-based methods
Confusion matrix metrics are threshold-dependent; full ROC curve not reported
Unspecified hyperparameters in original paper are undisclosed degrees of freedom in replication

critic_raw:
The SKS hate speech detection paper has several important methodological concerns.

1. The multi-task learning benefit (adding sentiment analysis) is evaluated without controlling for the total number of parameters and training examples. A model trained with sentiment labels has access to more supervision signal than a single-task model. The performance improvement from multi-task training could be entirely due to additional labeled data rather than to any information sharing between sentiment and hate speech detection. The paper should compare against a single-task model trained on equivalent total supervision (e.g., augmented hate speech labels via pseudo-labeling).

2. The category embeddings from a dictionary of derogatory words introduce a lexical lookup that is domain-specific. The paper claims performance improvements from category embeddings, but does not report whether the dictionary is aligned with the vocabulary of the evaluation datasets. If evaluation dataset vocabulary substantially overlaps with the dictionary, the improvement may reflect lexical memorization rather than learned semantic generalization. A test on out-of-vocabulary hate speech would distinguish these.

3. The multi-head attention mechanism in SKS applies attention over both text tokens and sentiment/category signals. The paper does not report which attention heads specialize in which signal type. Without this, it is impossible to verify that the model actually uses sentiment information to improve hate speech detection, as opposed to simply having a larger and more expressive model. Attention head specialization could be verified by ablating individual heads.

4. The benchmark datasets for hate speech detection (e.g., Founta et al., Davidson et al.) have known annotation artifacts: they were collected via keyword search for hate-related terms, creating lexical biases. Models that exploit these biases score well on the benchmark but fail on naturalistically collected hate speech. The paper does not test SKS on a dataset collected without keyword-based sampling.

5. Hate speech detection is a highly imbalanced classification problem (hate speech is typically a small minority of social media posts). The paper's reported accuracy metrics may be dominated by performance on the majority (non-hate) class. F1 score on the minority (hate) class and precision-recall curves are more informative than accuracy in this setting.
all_issues_raised: Multi-task benefit not controlled for additional supervision signal from sentiment labels
Category embedding improvement may reflect lexical memorization of dictionary vocabulary in evaluation data
No attention head specialization analysis to verify sentiment information is actually used for hate detection
Evaluation datasets collected via keyword search, creating lexical biases not tested with naturalistic data
Accuracy metrics dominated by majority class in imbalanced hate speech setting; F1 on hate class not emphasized
all_issues_adjudicated: Multi-task benefit not controlled for additional supervision signal from sentiment labels
Category embedding improvement may reflect lexical memorization of dictionary vocabulary in evaluation data
No attention head specialization analysis to verify sentiment information is actually used for hate detection
Evaluation datasets collected via keyword search, creating lexical biases not tested with naturalistic data
Accuracy metrics dominated by majority class in imbalanced hate speech setting; F1 on hate class not emphasized

critic_raw:
Fair Selective Classification via Sufficiency has several methodological issues.

1. The sufficiency criterion is implemented via a mutual information regularizer. Mutual information is notoriously difficult to estimate in high dimensions, and the estimator quality directly affects the fairness regularizer's behavior. The paper does not characterize the estimator bias or variance, or how sensitive the fairness-accuracy tradeoff is to the quality of the mutual information estimate. A poor estimator could produce a model that appears to satisfy sufficiency while actually violating it.

2. Selective classification (abstaining on uncertain samples) has a coverage-accuracy tradeoff: abstaining more improves accuracy on accepted samples but reduces coverage. The paper reports fairness metrics 'across the entire coverage scale,' but fairness at high abstention rates is less practically meaningful because the model only accepts easy, high-confidence samples. The claim that sufficiency holds across all coverage levels should be weighted by the practical operating region (moderate to high coverage).

3. The paper tests on four datasets, but fairness interventions are known to be highly dataset-specific. A method that reduces precision disparities on one dataset (e.g., income prediction) may increase other disparities on a different dataset (e.g., recidivism prediction). The paper should report whether the sufficiency regularizer consistently improves the same fairness criteria across all four datasets, not just on some of them.

4. The baseline selective classification model (without sufficiency regularization) is compared on fairness metrics, but the baseline may be making implicitly different abstention decisions than the sufficiency model. If the models abstain on different subsets of samples, the comparison is not directly controlled. A fair comparison would fix the abstention policy and measure fairness of the remaining accepted samples.

5. The 'may decrease overall accuracy' caveat is presented as acceptable given fairness improvement. However, the magnitude of accuracy-fairness tradeoff is dataset and subgroup-size dependent: for groups with very few samples, small changes in precision can produce large metric swings without meaningful model changes. The paper should report subgroup sample sizes and confidence intervals on fairness metrics to distinguish signal from noise.
all_issues_raised: Mutual information estimator quality not characterized; estimator bias may produce spurious sufficiency
Fairness at high abstention rates is less practically meaningful but given equal weight in evaluation
Sufficiency regularizer may not consistently improve all fairness criteria across all four test datasets
Abstention policies differ between baseline and proposed model, making fairness comparison uncontrolled
Subgroup sample sizes and confidence intervals on fairness metrics not reported
all_issues_adjudicated: Mutual information estimator quality not characterized; estimator bias may produce spurious sufficiency
Fairness at high abstention rates is less practically meaningful but given equal weight in evaluation
Sufficiency regularizer may not consistently improve all fairness criteria across all four test datasets
Abstention policies differ between baseline and proposed model, making fairness comparison uncontrolled
Subgroup sample sizes and confidence intervals on fairness metrics not reported

critic_raw:
FamNet's few-shot counting methodology has several important gaps.

1. The test-time adaptation strategy updates the network using exemplars from the test image. This is a form of transductive inference — the model sees the test input during 'adaptation.' If the adaptation is backpropagating gradients on the test image itself, then FamNet is not a true few-shot learner in the standard sense; it is performing test-time optimization, which has different computational and privacy properties and should be compared to other test-time optimization baselines (e.g., meta-learning methods that also use test-time gradient steps). The paper should clearly distinguish test-time optimization from held-out generalization.

2. The FSC-147 dataset is introduced by the same authors as the method. Self-introduced benchmark datasets can be inadvertently biased toward the proposed method's strengths: the image selection, annotation protocol, and difficulty calibration may reflect the assumptions embedded in FamNet's design. An independent evaluation on an existing counting dataset (e.g., ShanghaiTech for crowd counting, or COCO for object counting) would mitigate this concern.

3. The few-shot exemplars are crops from within the same image as the query. This is a significantly easier setting than few-shot learning across different images, because within-image exemplars share lighting conditions, scale, viewpoint, and occlusion patterns with the query regions. The paper does not report performance when exemplars are taken from reference images different from the query image.

4. The comparison to 'category-specific counting' methods is complicated by the fact that FamNet uses a class-agnostic approach while category-specific methods have class-specific priors. This comparison is not controlled for the amount of class information available: category-specific methods have seen many examples of the counted category during training, while FamNet has seen zero. A fair comparison would evaluate category-specific methods in a truly zero-shot setting or would provide FamNet with equivalent category-level training data.

5. Increasing the number of exemplars should reduce counting error — this is an implicit claim of the few-shot framework. The paper reports that error decreases with more exemplars but does not report the rate of decrease or whether it saturates. If error saturates quickly (e.g., at K=2 exemplars with K=3 providing no gain), the benefit of the few-shot framing is marginal and the method is essentially a one-shot approach.
all_issues_raised: Test-time adaptation is transductive inference on the test image, not held-out generalization; baseline comparison is inappropriate
FSC-147 benchmark is self-introduced; may be inadvertently biased toward FamNet's design assumptions
Within-image exemplars share scene properties with query regions, making the few-shot setting artificially easy
Comparison to category-specific methods conflates method type with available supervision
Exemplar count scaling behavior not characterized; saturation rate not reported
all_issues_adjudicated: Test-time adaptation is transductive inference on the test image, not held-out generalization; baseline comparison is inappropriate
FSC-147 benchmark is self-introduced; may be inadvertently biased toward FamNet's design assumptions
Within-image exemplars share scene properties with query regions, making the few-shot setting artificially easy
Comparison to category-specific methods conflates method type with available supervision
Exemplar count scaling behavior not characterized; saturation rate not reported
